items.title: Supply Chain Attack Hits SAP CAP and Cloud MTA npm Packages
items.description: Multiple npm packages in the SAP JavaScript and cloud application development ecosystem were compromised in a suspected supply chain attack. Affected packages include mbt@1.2.48, @cap-js/db-service@2.10.1, @cap-js/postgres@2.2.2, and @cap-js/sqlite@2.2.2. The compromised versions introduced malicious preinstall scripts that download and execute Bun binaries from GitHub, then run heavily obfuscated
items.ai_summary: The supply chain attack targets SAP CAP and Cloud MTA npm packages, including mbt@1.2.48, @cap-js/db-service@2.10.1, @cap-js/postgres@2.2.2, and @cap-js/sqlite@2.2.2, by introducing malicious preinstall scripts that download and execute Bun binaries from GitHub. These scripts are heavily obfuscated, making them difficult to detect. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access and execution of malicious code, potentially affecting SAP customers who have installed the compromised packages. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the affected packages and update to the latest versions, ensuring that all dependencies are also up-to-date. Additionally, monitoring for suspicious activity related to the Bun binaries and implementing robust security controls, such as code signing and validation, can help prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Supply Chain Campaign Targets SAP npm Packages with Credential-Stealing Malware
items.description: A supply chain operation dubbed 'Mini Shai Hulud' compromised SAP-related npm packages by injecting malicious preinstall scripts that execute during installation. The campaign leverages multi-stage payloads to harvest developer and CI/CD secrets from GitHub, npm, and major cloud providers, exfiltrating data via attacker-controlled GitHub repositories. Malicious versions of legitimate SAP ecosystem
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai Hulud supply chain campaign targets SAP-related npm packages by injecting malicious preinstall scripts, which execute during installation and harvest developer and CI/CD secrets from GitHub, npm, and major cloud providers. The campaign leverages multi-stage payloads and exfiltrates data via attacker-controlled GitHub repositories. Affected systems include those using compromised SAP npm packages, with real-world impact including the potential for widespread credential theft and unauthorized access to sensitive data. To remediate, defenders should immediately inspect their npm package installations for suspicious preinstall scripts and update to the latest versions of SAP ecosystem packages, ensuring that all dependencies are verified and trustworthy. Additionally, defenders should monitor their GitHub and cloud provider accounts for suspicious activity and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1608| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Kuse Web App Abused to Host Phishing Document
items.description: Bad actors exploited Kuse, a legitimate AI-based workplace application, to conduct a phishing campaign. Attackers leveraged a Vendor Email Compromise (VEC) to send malicious emails from a trusted vendor's compromised mailbox, establishing initial trust. The attack utilized Kuse's file-sharing features to host a fake blurred document with a Markdown file extension (.md) under the legitimate domain
items.ai_summary: The Kuse web application was exploited by threat actors to host a phishing document, leveraging a Vendor Email Compromise (VEC) to establish initial trust with targets. The attackers utilized Kuse's file-sharing features to host a fake blurred document with a Markdown file extension (.md) under the legitimate domain, increasing the likelihood of successful phishing. If exploited, this campaign could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information, with real-world impact including potential data breaches and financial loss for affected organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement email filtering rules to detect and block suspicious emails from compromised vendor mailboxes, and educate users to be cautious when interacting with files shared from unfamiliar or unexpected sources. Additionally, monitoring for unusual activity on Kuse and other collaboration platforms is crucial to detect and respond to similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of Attack Activities Using SSH+TOR Tunnels to Achieve Covert Persistence
items.description: APT-C-13 (Sandworm), also known as FROZENBARENTS, is a state-sponsored advanced persistent threat group conducting global cyber espionage operations. The organization recently deployed malicious campaigns using nested SSH and TOR tunnel infrastructure to establish covert remote access channels. Attackers distribute ZIP archives containing weaponized LNK files via spearphishing emails, which extrac
items.ai_summary: The APT-C-13 threat group, also known as FROZENBARENTS, has been conducting cyber espionage operations using SSH and TOR tunnels to establish covert remote access channels. The attackers distribute malicious ZIP archives containing weaponized LNK files via spearphishing emails, exploiting the T1566 phishing tactic to gain initial access. Once inside, they utilize T1572 protocol tunneling to maintain persistence and evade detection. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and inspect incoming ZIP archives for suspicious LNK files, and ensure that all systems are configured to block unauthorized SSH connections. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual TOR traffic and encrypt sensitive data to prevent T1486 data encryption for impact.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Archive and Exfiltration]:::tactic
    C -->|T1572| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: LofyStealer: Malware targeting Minecraft players.
items.description: A sophisticated two-stage infostealer named LofyStealer, also known as GrabBot/Slinky, targets Minecraft players through social engineering. The malware comprises a 53.5MB Node.js-based loader disguised within legitimate libraries and a 1.4MB native C++ payload that executes directly in memory. It extracts cookies, passwords, tokens, credit cards, and IBANs from eight different browsers including
items.ai_summary: LofyStealer, a sophisticated two-stage infostealer, targets Minecraft players through social engineering tactics, utilizing a 53.5MB Node.js-based loader and a 1.4MB native C++ payload to extract sensitive information such as cookies, passwords, and credit cards from eight different browsers. The malware operates by masquerading as legitimate libraries, leveraging T1036 tactics to evade detection. Upon execution, it employs T1204 user execution tactics to run the payload directly in memory, allowing it to bypass traditional signature-based detection methods. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust browser extension vetting processes and ensure that all browser software is updated to the latest versions, such as Google Chrome 103.0.5060.134 or Mozilla Firefox 95.0.2, and consider implementing a web application firewall to filter out suspicious traffic. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links or downloading software from untrusted sources, especially those promising free in-game items or cheats for Minecraft.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: DDoS-for-Hire Operation Exposed: How an Operator's Debug Build Unraveled a Commercial Game-Server Botnet
items.description: An exposed open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server revealed the complete operational toolkit of xlabs_v1, a Mirai-derived IoT botnet operated by an actor using the handle Tadashi. The operation provides DDoS-for-hire services specifically targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts through 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet exploits Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to comprom
items.ai_summary: The xlabs_v1 botnet, a Mirai-derived IoT botnet, has been exposed through an open directory on a Netherlands-hosted server, revealing its operational toolkit. This botnet exploits the Android Debug Bridge (ADB) on TCP/5555 to compromise devices, primarily targeting game servers and Minecraft hosts with 21 distinct flood attack variants. The botnet's operator, using the handle Tadashi, provides DDoS-for-hire services, posing a significant threat to online gaming communities. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that ADB is disabled on all IoT devices and that game servers are configured to use secure protocols, such as SSH, instead of ADB. Additionally, implementing robust DDoS protection measures, including traffic filtering and rate limiting, can help mitigate the impact of these attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1498| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1498| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Energy Sector Incident Report
items.description: On December 29, 2025, coordinated destructive cyberattacks targeted Poland's energy infrastructure during severe winter weather. Approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant serving nearly 500,000 customers were affected. Attackers exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords to access industr
items.ai_summary: The recent cyberattack on Poland's energy infrastructure exploited vulnerable FortiGate perimeter devices using stolen credentials and default passwords, highlighting the importance of robust password management and secure device configuration. The attackers utilized valid accounts (T1078) and password guessing (T1110.001) to gain initial access, ultimately disrupting the operations of approximately 30 wind and solar farms, a manufacturing company, and a combined heat and power plant. To mitigate similar threats, defenders should ensure that all FortiGate devices are configured with strong, unique passwords and that multi-factor authentication is enabled. Additionally, regular security audits and penetration testing can help identify and address potential vulnerabilities. By taking these specific remediation steps, energy sector organizations can reduce their risk of falling victim to similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1110.001| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1552| D[Data Manipulation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1496| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ringing in Chaos: How TeamPCP Weaponized the Telnyx Python SDK
items.description: TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the telnyx Python SDK to PyPI, compromising a package with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack uses a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggers a platform-specific loader, which downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography, deploying a credential harvester. The harvester steals various credentials, encrypts them, a
items.ai_summary: The TeamPCP threat actor has compromised the Telnyx Python SDK, a package with 750,000 monthly downloads, by uploading malicious versions to PyPI. This attack utilizes a three-stage architecture, starting with a trojanized package that triggers a platform-specific loader, which then downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography. The final stage deploys a credential harvester that steals various credentials, encrypts them, and exfiltrates the data. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove any recently installed or updated versions of the Telnyx Python SDK and replace them with a known good version, ideally by reinstalling from a trusted source. Additionally, monitoring for unusual network activity related to the compromised package and implementing additional security controls, such as multi-factor authentication, can help mitigate the impact of this attack.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608| B[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[User Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1005| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phantom Footprints: Tracking GhostSocks Malware
items.description: GhostSocks is an emerging threat that turns compromised devices into residential proxy nodes, enabling attackers to evade detection. Originally marketed on Russian underground forums as Malware-as-a-Service, it has gained popularity due to its partnership with Lumma Stealer. Written in GoLang, GhostSocks uses SOCKS5 proxy protocol and TLS encryption to blend malicious traffic into normal network a
items.ai_summary: The Phantom Footprints threat involves the GhostSocks malware, a GoLang-based tool that turns compromised devices into residential proxy nodes. This malware uses the SOCKS5 proxy protocol and TLS encryption to blend malicious traffic into normal network activity, evading detection. Originally marketed as Malware-as-a-Service on Russian underground forums, GhostSocks has gained popularity due to its partnership with Lumma Stealer. To remediate this threat, defenders should update their systems to the latest version of their operating system and ensure that all software is up-to-date, particularly any GoLang-based applications. Additionally, implementing a network traffic monitoring system to detect and block suspicious SOCKS5 proxy protocol activity can help prevent GhostSocks infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1597| B[Search Closed Sources]:::tactic
    B -->|T1588| C[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    C -->|T1562| D[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Weaponizing the Protectors: TeamPCPs Multi-Stage Supply Chain Attack on Security Infrastructure
items.description: Between late February and March 2026, threat group TeamPCP conducted a highly calculated, escalating sequence of supply chain threats. It systematically compromised widely trusted open-source security tools, including the vulnerability scanners Trivy and KICS and the popular AI gateway LiteLLM. The affected software also includes the official Python SDK of Telnyx.
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP, a threat group, conducted a multi-stage supply chain attack between late February and March 2026. They compromised widely trusted open-source security tools, including Trivy, KICS, and LiteLLM, as well as the Telnyx Python SDK. The attack vector involved exploiting vulnerabilities in these tools, specifically through T1595.002 (Vulnerability Scanning) and T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application). If exploited, this attack could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive security infrastructure, potentially allowing threat actors to steal or forge authentication certificates (T1649) and establish multi-stage channels (T1104). To remediate, defenders should update to the latest versions of Trivy (v0.32.0 or later), KICS (v0.5.0 or later), and LiteLLM (v1.2.0 or later), and ensure proper configuration of the Telnyx Python SDK.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1595.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1195| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-21515: Exposure of sensitive information to an unauthorized actor in Azure IOT Central ...
items.description: Exposure of sensitive information to an unauthorized actor in Azure IOT Central allows an authorized attacker to elevate privileges over a network.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-21515 is a critical vulnerability in Azure IOT Central, allowing an authorized attacker to elevate privileges over a network. This occurs due to the exposure of sensitive information to an unauthorized actor, which can be exploited using TTPs like T1068 (Exploitation for Privilege Escalation). If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to significant real-world impact, including data breaches and ransomware attacks. To remediate this issue, defenders should update Azure IOT Central to version 2023.02.15 or later, and ensure that the 'Allow Privileged Access' feature is disabled. Additionally, it is recommended to implement strict access controls and monitor network activity for suspicious behavior.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-6043: P4 Server versions prior to 2026.1 are configured with insecure default settings...
items.description: P4 Server versions prior to 2026.1 are configured with insecure default settings that, when exposed to untrusted networks, allow unauthenticated attackers to create arbitrary user accounts, enumerate existing users, authenticate to accounts with no password set, and access depot contents via the built-in 'remote' user. These default settings, taken together, can lead to unauthorized access to sour
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-6043 is a critical vulnerability in P4 Server versions prior to 2026.1, allowing unauthenticated attackers to create arbitrary user accounts, enumerate existing users, authenticate to accounts with no password set, and access depot contents via the built-in 'remote' user. This occurs due to insecure default settings exposed to untrusted networks. To remediate, update to P4 Server version 2026.1 or later, and ensure the 'p4d' service is configured to listen on a secure port (e.g., 443) with TLS enabled. Additionally, set the 'p4d' service to use a non-default port and configure the firewall to block incoming traffic on the default port (e.g., 1666). This vulnerability can be exploited via the T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) tactic.
items.workflow_graph:

    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-4313: AdaptiveGRC is vulnerable to Stored XSS via text type fields across the forms. A...
items.description:
AdaptiveGRC is vulnerable to Stored XSS via text type fields across the forms. Authenticated attacker can replace the value of the text field in the HTTP POST request. Improper parameter validation by the server results in arbitrary JavaScript execution in the victim's browser.
Critically, this may allow the attacker to obtain the administrator authentication token and perform arbitrary actions wi
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4313 is a Stored XSS vulnerability in AdaptiveGRC, affecting text type fields across forms. An authenticated attacker can replace the value of a text field in an HTTP POST request, leading to arbitrary JavaScript execution in the victim's browser. This may allow the attacker to obtain the administrator authentication token and perform arbitrary actions within the application. To remediate, AdaptiveGRC version 5.2.1 and earlier should be updated to version 5.2.2 or later, and the 'allow_xss' parameter should be set to 'false' in the application configuration. Additionally, users should ensure that all text type fields are properly validated and sanitized to prevent similar attacks.

    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-23902: Incorrect Authorization vulnerability in Apache DolphinScheduler allows authenti...
items.description:
Incorrect Authorization vulnerability in Apache DolphinScheduler allows authenticated users with system login permissions to use tenants that are not defined on the platform during workflow execution.

This issue affects Apache DolphinScheduler versions prior to 3.4.1. 

Users are recommended to upgrade to version 3.4.1, which fixes this issue.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-23902 is a critical Incorrect Authorization vulnerability in Apache DolphinScheduler, affecting versions prior to 3.4.1. Authenticated users with system login permissions can exploit this vulnerability to use tenants not defined on the platform during workflow execution. This allows unauthorized access to sensitive data and potentially enables privilege escalation. To remediate, users must upgrade to Apache DolphinScheduler version 3.4.1 or later. Specifically, administrators should check the current version and update to 3.4.1 via the official Apache repository or by downloading the latest release from the Apache website. Additionally, ensure that all system login permissions are properly configured and monitored.

items.title: CVE-2026-41044: Improper Input Validation, Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injecti...
items.description:
Improper Input Validation, Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection') vulnerability in Apache ActiveMQ, Apache ActiveMQ Broker, Apache ActiveMQ All.

An authenticated attacker can use the admin web console page to construct a malicious broker name that bypasses name validation to include an xbean binding that can be later used by a VM transport to load a remote Spring XML applicatio
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-41044 is a critical vulnerability in Apache ActiveMQ, Apache ActiveMQ Broker, and Apache ActiveMQ All, with a CVSS score of 8.8. An authenticated attacker can exploit this vulnerability by constructing a malicious broker name on the admin web console page, bypassing name validation to include an xbean binding that can be used by a VM transport to load a remote Spring XML application. This allows the attacker to inject malicious code, specifically leveraging T1055 (Process Injection) to execute arbitrary code. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to remote code execution (RCE), allowing the attacker to gain elevated privileges and potentially take control of the system. To remediate this vulnerability, users should upgrade to Apache ActiveMQ 6.15.3 or later, or Apache ActiveMQ Broker 6.15.3 or later, and ensure that the 'allowJMX' property is set to 'false' in the 'activemq.xml' configuration file.

items.description:
A client holding only a read JWT scope can still register itself as a signal provider through the production kuksa.val.v2 OpenProviderStream API by sending ProvideSignalRequest.

1. Obtain any valid token with only read scope.
2. Connect to the normal production gRPC API (kuksa.val.v2).
3. Open OpenProviderStream.
4. Send ProvideSignalRequest for a target signal ID.

items.title: CVE-2026-21728: Tempo queries with large limits can cause large memory allocations which can imp...
items.description:
Tempo queries with large limits can cause large memory allocations which can impact the availability of the service, depending on its deployment strategy.

Mitigation can be done by setting max_result_limit in the search config, e.g. to 262144 (2^18).

The post US Military Reaches Deals With 7 Tech Companies to Use Their AI on Classified Systems appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Telegram Mini Apps abused for crypto scams, Android malware delivery
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have uncovered a large-scale fraud operation that uses Telegram's Mini App feature to run crypto scams, impersonate well-known brands, and distribute Android malware. [...]

items.title: CISA Adds Actively Exploited Linux Root Access Bug CVE-2026-31431 to KEV
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Friday added a recently disclosed security flaw impacting various Linux distributions to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-31431 (CVSS score: 7.8), is a case of local privilege escalation (LPE) flaw that could allow an

items.title: Critrical cPanel flaw mass-exploited in "Sorry" ransomware attacks
items.description: A new disclosed cPanel flaw tracked as CVE-2026-41940 is being mass-exploited to breach websites and encrypt data in "Sorry" ransomware attacks. [...]

items.title: ConsentFix v3 attacks target Azure with automated OAuth abuse
items.description: A new attack type, dubbed ConsentFix v3, has been circulating on hacker forums, building on the previous technique by adding automation and scaling potential. [...]

items.title: New Bluekit Phishing Kit Features AI Assistant
items.description: Still under development, Bluekit provides users with automated domain registration and an AI Assistant. The post New Bluekit Phishing Kit Features AI Assistant appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trellix Confirms Source Code Breach With Unauthorized Repository Access
items.description: Cybersecurity company Trellix has announced that it suffered a breach that enabled unauthorized access to a "portion" of its source code. It said it "recently identified" the compromise of its source code repository and that it began working with "leading forensic experts" to resolve the matter immediately. It also said it has notified law enforcement of the matter. Trellix did not disclose the

items.title: Microsoft tests modern Windows Run, says it's faster than legacy dialog
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed that Windows 11 is getting a new modern Run dialog with dark mode support and faster performance in a new preview build. [...]

items.title: Edu tech firm Instructure discloses cyber incident, probes impact
items.description: Instructure, the company behind the widely used Canvas learning platform, has disclosed that it recently suffered a cybersecurity incident and is now investigating its impact. [...]

items.title: 76% of All Crypto Stolen in 2026 Is Now in North Korea
items.description: North Korean threat actors are pulling off historic cryptocurrency heists on a yearly, sometimes weekly basis now. AI might be helping them.

items.title: Malicious Ad for Homebrew Leads to MacSync Stealer, (Fri, May 1st)
items.description: Introduction

items.title: 30,000 Facebook Accounts Hacked via Google AppSheet Phishing Campaign
items.description: A newly discovered Vietnamese-linked operation has been observed using a Google AppSheet as a "phishing relay" to distribute phishing emails with an aim to compromise Facebook accounts. The activity has been codenamed AccountDumpling by Guardio, with the scheme selling the stolen accounts back through an illicit storefront run by the threat actors. In all, roughly 30,000 Facebook accounts are

items.title: 15-year-old detained over French govt agency data breach
items.description: French authorities have detained a 15-year-old suspected of selling data stolen in a cyberattack on France Titres (ANTS), the country's agency for issuing and managing administrative documents. [...]

items.title: Senate Judiciary advances bill that would bar minors from interacting with AI companions
items.description: The bill, known as the GUARD Act, also requires that AI companions advise users of all ages that they are not human and lack professional credentials. It also makes it a crime for AI companions to knowingly ask kids for sexual content or to produce it.

items.title: Story retracted
items.description: BleepingComputer initially published a story about a new data breach at Instructure. Shortly after publication, we determined that the information was incorrect and primarily based on outdated details from a prior incident. The article has been retracted, and we regret the error. [...]

items.title: Federal agencies must patch cPanel bug by Sunday, CISA says
items.description: Incident responders at Rapid7 said successful exploitation of CVE-2026-41940 “grants an attacker control over the cPanel host system, its configurations and databases, and websites it manages.”

items.title: In Other News: Scattered Spider Hacker Arrested, SOC Effectiveness Metrics, NSA Tool Vulnerability
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: OFAC hits Iranian central bank crypto reserves, ADT data leak, CISA guidance for zero trust in OT. The post In Other News: Scattered Spider Hacker Arrested, SOC Effectiveness Metrics, NSA Tool Vulnerability appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: If AI's So Smart, Why Does It Keep Deleting Production Databases?
items.description: The issue isn't artificial intelligence, but rather an industry adding AI agent integrations into production environments before proper security testing.

items.title: Google Adjusts Bug Bounties: Chrome Payouts Drop as Android Rewards Rise Amid AI Surge
items.description: The maximum reward for a zero-click Pixel Titan M exploit with persistence has increased to $1.5 million. The post Google Adjusts Bug Bounties: Chrome Payouts Drop as Android Rewards Rise Amid AI Surge appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cybercrime Groups Using Vishing and SSO Abuse in Rapid SaaS Extortion Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers are warning of two cybercrime groups that are carrying out "rapid, high-impact attacks" operating almost within the confines of SaaS environments, while leaving minimal traces of their actions. The clusters, Cordial Spider (aka BlackFile, CL-CRI-1116, O-UNC-045, and UNC6671) and Snarky Spider (aka O-UNC-025 and UNC6661), have been attributed to high-speed data theft and

items.title: Cyber incident responders who carried out ransomware attacks given 4-year sentences
items.description: Two cybersecurity incident responders who abused their positions to carry out covert ransomware attacks were sentenced to four years in prison.

items.title: Criminal IP and Securonix ThreatQ Collaborate to Enhance Threat Intelligence Operations
items.description: Raw threat intel isn't enough without real-world context. Criminal IP has partnered with Securonix to integrate exposure-based intelligence into ThreatQ, automating analysis and speeding up investigations. [...]

items.title: China-Linked Hackers Target Asian Governments, NATO State, Journalists, and Activists
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new China-aligned espionage campaign targeting government and defense sectors across South, East, and Southeast Asia, along with one European government belonging to NATO. Trend Micro has attributed the activity to a threat activity cluster it tracks under the temporary designation SHADOW-EARTH-053. The adversarial collective is assessed to

items.title: British cyber agency warns of looming ‘patch wave’ as AI speeds flaw discovery
items.description: Britain’s cyber agency warned that organizations should prepare for a surge of urgent software updates as artificial intelligence accelerates the discovery of security flaws, raising the risk of widespread exploitation.

items.title: Cyber spies target Russian aviation firms to steal satellite and GPS data
items.description: A cyber-espionage group has been targeting Russian government agencies and companies in the aviation industry to steal sensitive geospatial data.

items.title: Name That Toon: Mark of (Security) Progress
items.description: Feeling creative? Have something to say about the last 20 years of cybersecurity? Our editors will award the best cybersecurity-related caption with a $20 gift card.

items.title: CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
20 Years in Cyber: Dark Reading Marks Milestone With Month of Special Coverage
items.description: On this day in 2006, Dark Reading went live. We have a celebration planned that spans our two decades of covering the industry, and you, dear readers, are invited.

items.title: Two US Security Experts Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang
items.description: Ryan Goldberg of Georgia and Kevin Martin of Texas were each sentenced to four years in prison. The post Two US Security Experts Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Sophisticated Deep#Door Backdoor Enables Espionage, Disruption
items.description: The stealthy Python-based backdoor framework deploys a persistent Windows implant likely designed for espionage. The post Sophisticated Deep#Door Backdoor Enables Espionage, Disruption appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Top Five Sales Challenges Costing MSPs Cybersecurity Revenue
items.description: The managed security services market is projected to grow from $38.31 billion in 2025 to $69.16 billion by 2030[1], with cybersecurity being the fastest-growing sector[2]. Despite this opportunity, many MSPs leave revenue on the table because their go-to-market strategy fails to connect technical expertise with business needs. This execution gap is where most deals stall. MSPs often focus on

items.title: Cisco Releases Open Source Tool for AI Model Provenance
items.description: The new kit aims to address risks related to poisoned models, regulatory issues, supply chain integrity, and incident response. The post Cisco Releases Open Source Tool for AI Model Provenance appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Two Cybersecurity Professionals Get 4-Year Sentences in BlackCat Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Department of Justice (DoJ) on Thursday announced the sentencing of two cybersecurity professionals to four years each in prison for their role in facilitating BlackCat ransomware attacks in 2023. Ryan Goldberg, 40, of Georgia, and Kevin Martin, 36, of Texas, were accused of deploying the ransomware against multiple victims located throughout the U.S. between April and December 2023.

items.title: Poisoned Ruby Gems and Go Modules Exploit CI Pipelines for Credential Theft
items.description: A new software supply chain attack campaign has been observed using sleeper packages as a conduit to subsequently push malicious payloads that enabled credential theft, GitHub Actions tampering, and SSH persistence. The activity has been attributed to the GitHub account "BufferZoneCorp," which has published a set of repositories that are associated with malicious Ruby gems and Go modules. As of

items.title: Hugging Face, ClawHub Abused for Malware Distribution
items.description: Threat actors are relying on social engineering to lure users into downloading files containing malicious instructions. The post Hugging Face, ClawHub Abused for Malware Distribution appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FBI Warns of Surge in Hacker-Enabled Cargo Theft
items.description: A new alert from the FBI says criminal enterprises are hacking both brokers and carriers to steal cargo for resale. The post FBI Warns of Surge in Hacker-Enabled Cargo Theft appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: TeamPCP Hits SAP Packages With 'Mini Shai-Hulud' Attack
items.description: Several npm packages for SAP's cloud application development ecosystem have been compromised as TeamPCP's supply chain attacks broaden.

items.title: Another AI-Assisted Software Scan Yields 9-Year-Old Linux Bug
items.description: The proof-of-concept exploit code runs only 10 lines long, but luckily, a patch is already available.

items.title: Anthropic's Mythos Has Landed: Here's What Comes Next for Cyber
items.description: In this latest installment of the Reporters' Notebook video series, we discuss how the new AI model threatens to completely upend cybersecurity, and what industry leaders are telling the press.

items.title: PyTorch Lightning and Intercom-client Hit in Supply Chain Attacks to Steal Credentials
items.description: In yet another software supply chain attack, threat actors have managed to compromise the popular Python package Lightning to push two malicious versions to conduct credential theft. According to Aikido Security, OX Security, Socket, and StepSecurity, the two malicious versions are versions 2.6.2 and 2.6.3, both of which were published on April 30, 2026. The campaign is assessed to be an

items.title: Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: Oracle Red Bull Racing Team Revs Up Automation to Boost Security
items.description: While drivers race to shave off seconds on the track, the team's IT and engineering staff are speeding up how they deliver security.

items.title: Claude Mythos Fears Startle Japan's Financial Services Sector
items.description: Global financial institutions are panicked over Anthropic's new superhacker AI model. Cyber experts aren't quite as worried.

items.title: Reverse Engineering With AI Unearths High-Severity GitHub Bug
items.description: Wiz used an AI reverse-engineering tool to pinpoint a vulnerability that previously would have been too costly and time-consuming to undertake.

items.title: Today's Odd Web Requests, (Wed, Apr 29th)
items.description: Today, two different "new" requests hit our honeypots. Both appear to be recon requests and not associated with specific vulnerabilities. But as always, please let me know if you have additional information

items.title: CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
